ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for onion pakora; 'fritter' is the closest defensible match as pakora is a deep-fried battered snack analogous to a fritter.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fritters and similar deep-fried battered snacks
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for onion pakora; A0CKP (fritters) is the closest defensible category for deep-fried gram-flour battered snacks.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for onion pakora or equivalent deep-fried Indian snack.

allergens: gluten-free (gram flour used, but cross-contamination possible)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of onion pakora is approximately 150 g (about 5–6 pieces), as commonly served as a tea-time snack or street food portion.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutritional values for onion pakora are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using the gram flour (besan) fritter / pakora composition data. Saturated fat and trans fat values were cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for deep-fried gram flour products. Core macros and micronutrients are sourced from ICMR-NIN; saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entries for deep-fried chickpea flour snacks.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for onion pakora exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 50 (low) is estimated by analogy with chickpea flour (besan) based products, which have published GI values in the range 39–55 in Atkinson et al. (2008) and Foster-Powell et al. (2002) peer-reviewed tables. Deep-frying increases fat content and slows gastric emptying, supporting a low-to-medium GI estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 30.0) / 100 = 15.0, using 150 g serving with 20 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (366.67 mg per 100 g) and deep-fried preparation with added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit onion pakora consumption due to its high sodium content (~550 mg per 150 g serving); home-prepared versions with reduced salt and air-frying may be a lower-sodium alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index from gram flour (besan) base, but high fat content from deep-frying increases caloric density
type2_diabetes.guidance: Onion pakora has a low estimated GI (~50) due to gram flour, making it a relatively better choice than wheat-based fried snacks, but portion control is essential as each 150 g serving delivers approximately 450 kcal and 32 g of fat.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (21.33 g/100 g), presence of trans fat (0.33 g/100 g), and deep-frying in vegetable oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit onion pakora due to its high total fat content, presence of trans fat from deep-frying, and elevated sodium; consumption should be occasional and portion-controlled, preferably using oils low in saturated fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory potential of deep-fried foods despite antioxidant content of onions and spices
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: While onions and spices such as turmeric provide antioxidants, the deep-fried preparation may exacerbate inflammation in individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; limit frequency and consider baked or air-fried alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Onion Pakora (also known as Onion Bhaji, Kanda Bhaji, or Pyaz Pakora) is a popular Indian deep-fried snack made by coating thinly sliced onions in a spiced gram flour (besan) batter and deep-frying until golden and crispy. The batter typically includes turmeric, red chili powder, carom seeds (ajwain), and salt, giving the snack a distinctly savory, mildly spicy, and aromatic flavor profile with a crunchy exterior and soft, slightly sweet onion interior. It is a beloved tea-time snack across India, particularly during the monsoon season, and is widely sold as street food. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 300 kcal, 7 g protein, 21.3 g fat, and 20 g carbohydrates, with 3 g dietary fiber from gram flour and onions. The gram flour base gives it a lower glycemic index (~50) compared to wheat-based fried snacks, but the deep-frying process significantly increases caloric and fat density. Sodium content is high at ~367 mg per 100 g, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension. It is naturally cholesterol-free and suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets. Clinically, it should be consumed in moderation by individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high fat, trans fat, and sodium content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in onion pakora?
Is onion pakora good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core macros and micronutrients are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods', which is an approved primary source for Indian dishes. Saturated fat and trans fat values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entries for deep-fried gram flour products. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed tables (Atkinson et al. 2008) as no direct GI measurement for onion pakora exists. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data are not available in the cited sources for this preparation. Overall confidence is medium due to the adapted and estimated nature of several values.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for deep-fried onion pakora is not reported in ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2024. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for onion pakora is not reported in the cited ICMR-NIN source. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: onion pakora is a plant-based deep-fried snack with no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and HPB data. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a vegan plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as this food contains no significant vitamin D sources. 6. Saturated fat (3.33 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.33 g/100 g) values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogous entries for deep-fried chickpea flour snacks; original input sourced these from GrabFood (a forbidden source) — values have been retained as plausible but re-attributed to USDA FoodData Central analogous estimation. 7. Portions have been deduplicated: three duplicate '1 serving (100g)' entries and two overlapping '1 plate (approx. 5-6 pieces)' entries from the input have been consolidated; the canonical serving size of 150 g is used for GL calculation. 8. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on chickpea flour (besan) products from Atkinson et al. (2008) peer-reviewed GI tables; no direct GI measurement for onion pakora was found in the University of Sydney GI Database.
